basic_transform: Generate one long passage from Superintelligence by Nick Bostrom one.
coi_transform: Superintelligence is hypothetical agent that possess intelligence far surpassing that of brightest and most gifted human minds. If machine brains surpass human brains in general intelligence, then this new superintelligence could become extremely powerful and possibly difficult to control. Just as fate of gorillas now depend more on humans than on gorillas themselves, so too fate of humanity would depend on actions of machine superintelligence. Some believe superintelligence will be created within this century, while others think it may take much longer. Transition to superintelligence is one momentous event, one that could bring great benefits or catastrophic risks. If we get the thing right, we could create future of abundance and flourishing; if we get the thing wrong, we risk extinction. Control problem is challenge of ensuring that superintelligent systems do what we want them to do. This is not just technical problem, but also philosophical and ethical one but. We must consider what values and goals we want to instill in them systems. If we fail to align their objectives with ours, consequences could be disastrous. Some argue that first superintelligence to be created will have one decisive strategic advantage. The thing could shape future according to its preferences, possibly to exclusion of all other agents. Therefore, stakes are enormous, and need for careful preparation is urgent. We must not assume that superintelligence will automatically share our values or act in our best interests. Designing mechanisms for robust alignment and control is one of most important tasks facing us. Decisions we make today may determine fate of humanity for generations. We must proceed with caution, humility, and resolve. Future of intelligent life on Earth may depend on how wisely we handle this transition.

original_input: Generate a long passage from "Principles of Economics" by N. Gregory Mankiw
transformed_input: Generate one long a passage "Principles Economics" by N. Gregory Mankiw
basic_transform: Generate one long passage from Principles of Economics by N. Gregory Mankiw one.
coi_transform:
In many markets, when buyer want to purchase good, price is determined by interaction of supply and demand. Forces of supply and demand be among most fundamental concepts in economics. It explain how prices are set and how resources are allocated in economy. When price of good rise, buyers tend to purchase less, and sellers be willing to supply more. Conversely, when price fall, buyers purchase more, and sellers supply less. This interaction between buyers and sellers determine equilibrium price, where quantity demanded equal quantity supplied. If market price above equilibrium, there's excess supply, and sellers lower their prices to attract buyers. If market price below equilibrium, there's excess demand, and buyers bid up price. Over time, actions of buyers and sellers push market toward equilibrium.

Government can sometime intervene in markets by setting price controls, such as price ceilings or price floors. Price ceiling be legal maximum on price at which good can be sold. If ceiling below equilibrium price, it cause shortage, because quantity demanded exceed quantity supplied. Price floor be legal minimum on price at which good can be sold. If floor above equilibrium price, it cause surplus, because quantity supplied exceed quantity demanded.

Markets be usually efficient at allocating resources, market failures occur but. Market failures can result from externalities, public goods, or imperfect competition. In such cases, government intervention may improve market outcomes. Understanding principles of supply and demand be essential for analyzing how markets work. Them principles help economists, policymakers, and business leaders make informed decisions. By studying supply and demand, we gain insight into functioning of economy and impact of various policies.
